coredata.dc:title: Aligned graphene array anodes with dendrite-free behavior for high-performance Li-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Chang
Dong, Lei
Zheng, Nan
Zhu, Haiyin
Wu, Cong
Zhao, Fugang
Liu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Low rate-performance and safety issue associated with lithium dendrite plating under abuse conditions seriously limit the further application of graphite-based anode for lithium storage. Here, we report a high-performance, dendrite-free anode enabled by highly-ordered graphene arrays. This rationally designed anode consists of periodically arranged graphene sheets and interlayer-pillared carbon dots. This resultant anode performs a specific capacity of 64.5 mAh g−1 at high current density of 6 C, ~4.5 times higher than the commercial graphite anode counterpart. More importantly, the resultant anode performs lithium dendrite-free behavior even after over-lithiation, indicating its fast Li-ion transport kinetics and high structural stability. The current work provides a simple and effective strategy on electrode materials for fast charging and safe LIBs towards practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aligned graphene array
Low tortuosity
Dendrite-free
Fast charging
Safe anode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AGA-C and AGA electrodes
Fabrication of CG electrodes and PG electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFA0210600), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21805185) and Shanghai Rising-Star Program (20QA1406600). We also acknowledge Center for High-resolution Electron Microscopy (CℏEM), SPST of ShanghaiTech University (#EM02161943) for support.

The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program ( 2019YFA0210600 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21805185 ) and Shanghai Rising-Star Program ( 20QA1406600 ). We also acknowledge Center for High-resolution Electron Microscopy (CℏEM), SPST of ShanghaiTech University (#EM02161943) for support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure schematic illustration of the comparison between CG and AGA-C. (a, b) Schematic diagram of over-plating lithium on CG electrodes (a) and AGA-C electrodes (b). (c, d) Simulation results of Li-ion concentration in CG electrode (c) and AGA-C electrode (d) under 1 mA cm−2 current density. Here, colour in (c and d) denotes Li-ion concentration, the white lines represent graphene sheets, black lines show ion-flux streamlines.

Phase structure analysis of CG, PG and AGA-C. (a) XRD patterns of samples. Full width at half maximum (FWHM) of (002) peaks is marked, and scale bar showing the peak intensity is also indicated. (b) Raman spectrum of samples, intensity ratio of D-band versus G-band was labelled.

Morphological characterization of AGA-C electrode. (a, b) top-view SEM image of the as-prepared AGA-C electrode. Inset image of (a) shows the corresponding photo of the electrode pellet. (c) Cross-sectional view SEM image of AGA-C. (d) top-view SEM image of AGA-C electrode after charge/discharge 500 cycles at C-rate of 1 C. (e) HRTEM image of AGA. (f) SAED pattern of AGA. (g) TEM image of AGA-C, the inset plot is size-distribution of the deposited carbon nanodots. (h) HRTEM image of carbon nanodots.

(a-d) Simulated Li-ion concentration in AGA-C (a-c) and CG (d) electrodes under different current density of 1, 2, 4 and 6 mA cm−2. Differently-arranged AGA-C worms were studied, 90o (a), 45o (b) and 10o (c).

Over-lithiation measurements of Li||CG cells and Li||AGA-C cells at different conditions, the insets are photos of corresponding electrodes. (a-j)SEM images of CG (a,c,e,f,i) and AGA-C (b,d,f,h,j) electrode after over-lithiation test with different current densities (1~5 mA cm−2) and different capacities (1~5 mAh cm−2).

Electrochemical performances of AGA-C compared with CG. (a) Nyquist plots of Li||AGA-C and Li||CG half cells before and after 1 cycle. The inset shows the equivalent circuit. (b) The GCD curves of AGA-C at the C-rate of 0.1 C. (c) Rate capability of CG and AGA-C. (d) The comparison of areal capacity versus current density of AGA-C electrodes with published results. (e) Long cycling performances of CG and AGA-C with various mass loading weights at the C-rate of 1 C.

head.ce:title: Aligned graphene array anodes with dendrite-free behavior for high-performance Li-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Physical Science and Technology, ShanghaiTech University, Shanghai 201210, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Low rate-performance and safety issue associated with lithium dendrite plating under abuse conditions seriously limit the further application of graphite-based anode for lithium storage. Here, we report a high-performance, dendrite-free anode enabled by highly-ordered graphene arrays. This rationally designed anode consists of periodically arranged graphene sheets and interlayer-pillared carbon dots. This resultant anode performs a specific capacity of 64.5 mAh g−1 at high current density of 6 C, ~4.5 times higher than the commercial graphite anode counterpart. More importantly, the resultant anode performs lithium dendrite-free behavior even after over-lithiation, indicating its fast Li-ion transport kinetics and high structural stability. The current work provides a simple and effective strategy on electrode materials for fast charging and safe LIBs towards practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have been widely used for portable electronics and are promising in electric vehicles (EVs) [1–6]. However, their low charging rate hampers the developments for the applications of EVs comparing with that of traditional gasoline cars. Hence, developing fast-charging materials is one of the most important research directions [7–9]. A fast-charging project is proposed by the US Advanced Battery Consortium, the goal is charging for 80% of the capacity in 15 mins [3]. Nowadays, one of the main components that limits the rate capability of commercial LIBs is graphite anode. It shows a poor insertion/extraction kinetics of lithium ions especially at high current density, which restricts the Li-ion diffusion and causes the poor rate performance. Recently, researchers have proposed many strategies to deal with these problems and have made some good progresses, such as increasing density of active sites [10–12], constructing 3D stereoscopic structure [7,8,13,14] and reducing the electrode tortuosity [2,15,16]. Studart et al. present a strategy to make aligned graphite electrodes in the rotating magnetic field for high rate performance Li-ion batteries [2]. Zhang et al. report a three-dimensional (3D) edge-curled graphene anode to prevent the aggregation of graphene sheets and form stacked pores for fast charging LIBs [8].

While, safety concern of graphite-based anode under extreme conditions is often overlooked. Metallic Li platting on the graphite anode can be susceptible during fast charging rate, low temperatures or over charging. This is because of the low lithium intercalation potential (~100 mV, close to Li deposition potential) and the reduction in the intercalation kinetics [17–22]. As a result, Li dendrites may keep growing on surface of graphite, which subsequently leads to safety issue and loss of reversible Li for the state-of-the-art LIBs. On the contrary, an ideal anode without Li dendrites formation by over-charging is able to store the platted lithium metal, which increases the total capacity of anode. Additionally, a number of graphene-derived materials has been reported aiming to take the place of graphite towards fast charging Li-ion batteries [8,23–25]. However, they usually exhibit approximately linear charging-discharging curves, unlike graphite anode which has voltage plateau. And in most publications, graphene electrodes were usually tested in a very wide cut-off voltage ranged from 0.01 to 3.0 V in half cell, giving relatively high specific capacities. Nonetheless, such enhanced performance is not practical and available in full Li-ion cells. It is recommended that the charging upper limit should be lower than 1.5 V for anode materials [26–28].

tightHerein, we report a high-performance, safe anode on the basis of aligned graphene array (AGA). The AGAs comprised of periodically-arranged graphene sheets facilitate fast ion-transport also result in Li dendrite-free nature even under over-lithiation condition. Also, carbon nanodots are introduced into the interlayer spacing of adjacent graphene sheets as “pillars” in order to avoid the re-stack of face-to-face arranged graphene sheets in AGA. Due to the low-tortuosity and stable structure, the resultant AGA-C anode show significantly improved rate performance and cycling stability, compared to the commercial graphite (CG) counterparts. Importantly, Li dendrites growth can be largely suppressed in AGA-C at Li deposited potential, otherwise it could penetrate the separator, resulting in short-circuiting eventually.

The fabrication process is illustrated in Fig. S1. Taking pristine graphite flakes (PG) as raw materials, AGA was synthesized by an interlayer expansion reaction based our previous work [29,30]. The mild, interlayer reaction result in a sharp increase of specific surface area and the gas-releasing process make the graphite flakes expand along their c-direction, giving accordion-like AGA worms. In previous work, we have demonstrated that AGA have graphene-like properties, far better than the common thermally-expanded graphite [29,31]. The SEM images of PG and AGA were given in Fig. S2. Obviously, AGA shows layered structure with observable interlayer channels. Also, these layers in AGA can be easily exfoliated into separated graphene sheets by slight physical forces [29]. Then, AGA was dispersed into glucose solution and keep it in autoclave at 120°C for 5 h. After nature cooling, the processed solution was poured into vacuum filter and added a small amount of graphene oxide solution as conductive agent and binder, giving a gel-like AGA-glucose film. Press it by using a smooth metallic plate and then we acquire a freestanding film. After vacuum freeze drying and carbonization in Ar atmosphere, the AGA-glucose film turned into AGA-C electrode finally. This method can not only form the free-standing and binder-free electrode (without Cu current collector and polymer binder), but also reduce the electrode tortuosity and shorten transport pathway.

The structure comparison of the AGA-C with CG is depicted in Fig. 1 a-b. CG consists of disordered graphene sheets with high tortuosity, which therefore results in poor kinetic process and restricts the Li-ion diffusion (Fig. 1a). When over-lithiation, Li dendrites can plate on the graphite surface because of the slow intercalation kinetics and the strong heterogeneous distribution of Li-ion [32]. In contrast, the AGA-C anode with aligned, highly-periodic graphene sheets could build fast diffusion channels and homogenize Li-ion flux distribution. Then Li metal plates in the pores of graphene sheets and then continuous on the surface without the Li dendrites formation at over-lithiation condition as shown in figure 1b. In addition, most graphene materials have a strong tendency for adjacent sheets to converge together because of the π-π stacking. In our work, carbon nanodots were introduced as “pillar” into the interlayer, in order to physically restrain the restacking of the graphene sheets and strengthen the structural stability of aligned graphene arrays during cycling [33,34].

To further demonstrate the crucial roles of the aligned structure, we modelled Li ion transport behaviour and Li-ion distribution in different current density and electrode configurations by COMSOL Multiphysics software (see below for more details) [30,35]. The traditional graphite configuration (Fig. 1c) displays longer ion transport distance, slower diffusion velocity and stronger concentration gradient, which limit its rate capability in LIBs. High Li-ion concentration can be seen in the superficial layer (hot spot area), which may cause the growth of Li dendrites. While, AGA-C is able to reduce tortuosity and shorten the ion transport pathway because of the aligned channels, therefore the Li-ion concentration distribution is homogeneous on this electrode as shown in Fig. 1d. As a result, during over-lithiation, AGA-C can restrain the formation of lithium dendrites and eliminate the potential safety risks, while for CG, lithium dendrites are difficult to avoid.

Next, the phase structures of various samples including CG, PG and AGA-C were studied. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra (Fig. 2 a) demonstrates the typical sharpest diffractions peak at 2θ ≈ 26.4° and 54.5°, which belong to the (002) and (004) crystal faces of graphene (PDF# 41-1487). The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of CG, PG and AGA-C were 0.491, 0.147 and 0.793, respectively. A much larger FWHM of (002) peak can be seen for AGA-C than that for PG and CG. Moreover, the intensity of peaks was labelled by using scale bar, AGA-C exhibited the weakest (002) peak in comparison to CG and PG. The phenomenon could be attributed to the dramatical change of the layer structure of samples, such as expansion of interlayer spacing, decrease of graphite structure and formation of surface defects in graphene sheets. In addition, Raman spectroscopy was used to indicate the structures of above samples. One peak located at ~1350 cm−1 (D-band) ascribed to defects and adhering oxygen-containing groups on the surface and disordered carbon, and another peak at ~1575 cm−1 (G-band) corresponding to the sp2-hybridized carbon with graphite structure can be observed obviously as given in Fig. 2b. The intensity ratio of ID /IG represents the change of structure and degree of disorder. The intensity ratio increased from 0.16 to 1.22 after the preparation process from PG to AGA-C, which is resulted from the deposited carbon nanodots with more sp3 hybridized carbon [36]. Moreover, the surface chemical composition of AGA-C and CG was investigated with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As exhibited in Fig. S4, CG has very slightly oxidized surface while the surface of AGA-C was oxidized apparently. It proves that the graphene surface in AGA-C enriched with defects and oxygen-containing groups [37,38]. Specific surface area (SSA) test shows an improved value of 22 m2 g−1 for AGA-C, lager than that of the AGA precursor (6.5 m2 g−1) and CG (3.0 m2 g−1). Such SSA facilitate ion-transport in electrode but avoid the formation of excessive solid electrolyte interphase film during electrochemical cycles.

The morphology of the samples was characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 3 a, most graphene sheets in the AGA-C electrode are ordered and along the vertical direction, which can guarantee a low tortuosity and a shorter ion transport distance. It can be seen that the electrode consisted of accordion-like AGA-C arrays, and the diameter of one array is ~100 μm. Furthermore, the ordered structure keeps well after 500 cycles (Fig. 3c). The cross-sectional SEM images of AGA-C electrodes was displayed in Fig. 3d. The graphene (010) crystal plane was observed from high resolution TEM (HRTEM) and the interplanar spacing of (010) plane is 0.24 nm (Fig. 3e), demonstrating unbroken crystal structure of graphene. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3f) exhibits the typical diffraction spots of hexagonal system of pristine graphene. It can be observed from Fig. 3g that the carbon nanodots were distributed uniformly on graphene layer for AGA-C. Inset of Fig. 3g expresses the nanodots size distribution plot ranged from 8 to 20 nm. HRTEM image in Fig. 3h shows the typical (002) crystal plane of graphite, indicating the “pillar” structure of inserted carbon nanodots.

For further prove the well aligned structure of AGA-C, the tortuosity measurement was carried out. Tortuosity (τ) is a ratio that characterizes the convoluted pathways of Li+ diffusion in the electrolyte phase of a porous electrode, defined as[39-41]
(1)
τ =
ε
D 0
D eff
where ε is the volume fraction of the conductive phase (in the present case ε is the porosity of the electrode materials), and D0 and Deff are intrinsic and effective Li+ diffusivity, respectively. The porosity (ε) was calculated by the equation:
(2)
ε
= 1 −
ρ 1
ρ 0
where ε is porosity, ρ0 is theory density of graphite (2.25 g cm−3), ρ1 is the actual density. The weight and volume of electrode films were measured by using analytical balance micrometer. Mass divided by volume, giving the actual density (ρ1 ). Finally, the porosity (ε) was obtained.

The tortuosity measurement of electrode materials (without current collectors) usually is obtained from two methods: DC-polarization (also named polarization interrupt) and AC-impedance [39]. Here we measure the tortuosity of CG and AGA-C electrodes by DC-polarization method in the symmetric cell (Fig. S5a). As shown in Fig. S5b-d, ln|U(t) − U(t = ∞)| versus t plots showing the depolarization processes for various symmetric cells. A linear fit of these data was built and then the characteristic relaxation time of the depolarization process (tδ ) was obtained from the slope of the plot in the linear fitting range. The Li+ diffusivity (D) could be calculated according to the following equation: [39–41]
(3)
t δ
=
L 2
π
2
D
where π is constant, L is the thickness of the PTFE ring when measuring the intrinsic diffusivity of the electrolyte (D0 ) and the thickness of CG or AGA-C electrode when determining the effective diffusivity (Deff ). The intrinsic diffusivity of the electrolyte is measured to be 4.90 × 10−7 cm2 s−1 and effective diffusivity of CG and AGA-C are 5.18 × 10−8 cm2 s−1 and 2.45 × 10−7 cm2 s−1, respectively. By measuring and calculating, the porosity of CG and AGA-C are determined to be 0.40 and 0.85, respectively. Here the tortuosity is calculated using equation (1) with above data. The AGA-C electrode shows a reduced tortuosity of 1.70, compared with the control sample of 3.78, which confirms the aligned structure.

For further explore the aligned structure in details, we employ COMSOL Multiphysics software to simulate the resultant electrode under compression. As show in Figure S3, the included angle between graphene sheet and substrate represents the differently-arranged AGA-C worms in electrode or the structural distortion under compression and they are named AGA-C-90, AGA-C-45, AGA-C-10 and CG. In Fig. 4
, the colour mappings exhibit lithium-ion concentration after discharging under different current density ranging from 1-6 mA cm−2. The arrows show the transport pathways of lithium ions and the colour intensity represents the corresponding local ion-concentration. On behalf of electrolyte, the initial ion-concentration of the whole system is set to 1000 mol m−3. As a result, the concentration different between the top surface and bottom (ΔC, as called concentration polarization) reflects the extent of ion transport in electrodes. For example, at current density of 1 mA cm−2, AGA-C shows homogeneous ion concentration for all the orientations (with the included angel from 90° to 10°). In contrast, ΔC of CG increases to 1171.2 mol m−3, over four times higher than that AGA-C. For higher current density, concentration polarizations of AGA-C increase slowly, still far lower than that for CG counterparts. Note that the included angel of 10o is quite small in AGA, which suggests nearly paralleled graphene sheets to substrate. Hence, the above results demonstrate that, for AGA based electrodes, the aligned structure facilitate fast ion-transport kinetics and lower ion concentration gradient in system, even under a large content of compression.

In order to prove the simulation results, we also measured electrochemical performance of AGA-C after compression with different extent. With the increase of pressure (from 0 to 5 MPa), the thickness of AGA-C anode reduces and the corresponding include angle becomes smaller. As shown in Figure S6a-b, after treated by 5 MPa of pressure, the thickness reduced from 126 μm to 76 μm. The later value is close to the thickness of commercial graphite flakes which tend to form a face-to-face stacking structure. Figure S6c-d show the corresponding electrochemical performance of compressed AGA-C anode. The compression can hardly affect the mass specific capacity of AGA-C. Interestingly, compression even increase the volumetric specific capacity because of the reduced electrode thickness. Comparing with CG electrode, AGA-C electrodes display higher specific capacity and volume specific capacity, which shows the advantage of volume energy density and practical value.

Avoiding lithium dendrites formation on graphite anode in LIBs is of great importance, especially for extreme conditions (e.g. over-lithiation) in practical use [17,18,32]. In order to observe the dendrites formation and growth, a series of over-lithiation measurements were carried out for half cells of electrode material coupled with Li metal [21,42]. Here, the cells were discharged to 0 V and then study the lithium metal plating behaviour. As shown in Fig. 4, lithium dendrites can be easily identified in CG but hardly observed in AGA-C under different current densities ranging from 1 to 5 mA cm−2 and a lithiation capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. While for higher capacity of 3 and 5 mA cm−2, lithium dendrites continuously increase in CG. In contrast, only homogenous lithium deposition was observed in AGA-C. These results agree well with the simulation results that aligned structure facilitate fast ion transport and low ion concentration gradient in electrode, thereby avoiding lithium dendrites formation.

Next, the electrochemical behaviours of CG and AGA-C have been further explored. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for the cells with graphite electrode coupled with Li metal were carried out. Fig. 6 a presents the Nyquist plots of Li||AGA-C and Li||CG half cells before and after 1 cycle, in which intersection with the real axis at the high frequencies corresponds to the electrolyte resistance (R0), the semicircle confirms the overlapping of the passivation film resistance (R1) and charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the straight line at low frequencies represents the Warburg diffusion of Li+ in the electrodes (Wo) [4,37,38]. It is clear that R1 + Rct of AGA-C is larger than CG before cycle, which is because AGA-C has a larger contact area with electrolyte and form thick passivation film during the standing time. After 1 cycle, R1 + Rct of AGA-C is smaller than that of CG. This result denotes the uniform passivation film after cycling and fast charge transfer process in AGA-C electrode, which is conducive to improve rate capability.

Furthermore, the galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) test was performed with the voltage cut-off range of 0.01-1.5 V (Fig. 6b). It should be noted that ~70% of capacity was presented below 0.5 V in the GCD curves, unlike other graphene-derivates showing an approximately linear charging-discharging curves. It is noticed in the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves (Fig. S7) that there is markedly difference between the first cycle and subsequent cycles. In the first half scanning cycle (from 1.5 to 0.01 V), the curve shows a large and broad reduction peak, ranging from 0.8 to 0.01 V, and the peak diminishes in the following cycles. This phenomenon can be ascribed to formation of SEI film and side reaction in the first cycle, which leads to extra capacity loss [19,43,44]. The rate capability and long cycling performance for the electrodes with and without carbon nanodots are firstly compared and shown in Fig. S8 and S9 (see Section 4.2 preparation processes). The AGA-C electrodes present higher capacity and better rate performance duo to the “pillar effect” of carbon nanodots, which strengthen the structural stability of aligned graphene arrays during cycling. Moreover, we conducted some experiments to explain the function of hydrothermal treatment in AGA-C preparation and characterize its necessity. As shown in Figure S10, AGA-C performs far better than the control sample without hydrothermal treatment. Note that the control sample was also treated by immersing in glucose aqueous solution for 5 h, the above result demonstrate that hydrothermal treatment facilitates the effective entrance of glucose aqueous solution into the interlayer spacing of AGA. This could be due to the nano-/micro-scale, 2D interlayer channels between adjacent graphene layers in AGA which is adverse for natural diffusion of aqueous solution. The hydrothermal treatment facilitates the entrance of glucose aqueous solution into the interlayer spacing of AGA and thereby the formation of intercalated carbon nanodots. We also take the PG electrode in a half-cell to test the long cycling performances at 1 C. It shows a poor specific capacity of ~20 mAh g−1 (Fig. S11), which illustrates that raw PG without parallel channel is not suitable for LIB anode.

In addition, the rate capability and cycling performance of CG and AGA-C with different mass loadings (1 C=372 mA g−1) have been studied systematically (Fig. 6c). We can see that the AGA-C electrodes (3 mg cm−2) exhibit higher specific capacities of 64.5 mAh g−1 at the high C-rate of 6 C, while the CG electrodes only deliver 14.3 mAh g−1. The good rate performance is attributed to short diffusion distance and fast charge transfer process due to aligned structure. Long cycling performances of CG and AGA-C with different mass loadings at 1 C is demonstrated in Fig. 6e. The AGA-C electrodes show an initial specific capacity of 188.6 mAh g−1 (mass loading=3 mg cm−2) following an activated process in the first a few tens cycles. It exhibits a high specific capacity of 115.4 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles, which is higher than the control sample of 84.9 mAh g−1. Furthermore, we conduct a pre-reactivation process in order to prove the electrochemical stability of AGA-C anode. Here, cells were put overnight after assemble to ensure the electrodes were wetted fully. Prior to the cycling test at 1 C, a reactivation galvanostatic charge and discharge test (0.1 C for 5 cycles and 0.5 C for 5 cycles) was performed. As shown in Figure S12, the capacity of AGA-C is stable at the first 100 cycles at 1 C and slowly decays from 100 to 500 cycles. While for CG, its capacity decays in first 7 cycles at 1 C and maintains in a relatively low value of 50~75 mAh g−1. The superior cycle performance of AGA-C is attributed to the aligned graphene sheets during cycling. Furthermore, the electrode with high mass loading (10 mg cm−2) was also fabricated and it exhibits a high specific capacity of 87.8 mAh g−1, while CG only displays 23.5 mAh g−1. Fig. 6d displays the plot of areal capacity vs. areal current density for comparing our work with published data[2, 8, 10, 24, 44]. Comparatively, the AGA-C electrodes deliver higher areal capacity than most reported values. Moreover, the full cells were activated at 0.1 C for 5 cycles following by a long cycle test at 0.5 C, AGA@C|LFP full cells show higher specific capacity and capacity retention relative to the control samples (Fig. S13). Hence, AGA-C with electrode-scaled aligned graphene sheets without restacking can provide fast ion transport kinetics and uniform ion distribution, resulting in the good electrochemical performances.

In summary, we have designed a new carbon-based anode for high-performance, safe LIBs. The AGA-C anode consisted of aligned graphene arrays reinforced by the intercalated carbon nanodots. The tortuosity of AGA-C anode was measured to be 1.70, far less than 3.78 for CG counterpart. In rate performance test, AGA-C electrodes displayed a high specific capacity of 64.5 mAh g−1 under a high current density of 6 C, 4.5 times higher than CG counterpart (14.3 mAh g−1). Moreover, for long-term cycles, AGA-C exhibited a high specific capacity of 188.6 mAh g−1 at 1 C with mass loading of 3 mg cm−2, and even exhibits 87.8 mAh g−1 with high mass loading 10 mg cm−2. Notably, AGA-C showed dendrite-free behavior even after over-lithiation. These results demonstrate AGA-C as high-performance, safe anode due to its well-designed structure having low tortuosity and high stability.

AGA was synthesized by an interlayer expansion reaction we reported previously.[29,30] In a typical experiment, 2 g of graphite flakes (Sigma, 100 mesh) and 17 g of CrO3 (Aladdin) were added into 14 mL of hydrochloric acid. Then the mixture was stirred for 2 h at room temperature and washed by water to obtain intercalated graphite. A controllable, interlayer gas-releasing reaction continued at room temperature for 2 days by taking the resultant intercalated graphite into 2 L of hydrogen peroxide (Adamas-beta®). Fresh AGA suspension was obtained by washing and natural settling for several times until pH=6-7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AGA-C and AGA electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: AGA-C electrode. Take 5 mL AGA suspension (~10 mg mL-1) into 50 mL 0.1 M glucose solution and stir well. Then the mixture was perform hydrothermal treat in hydrothermal reactor at 120°C for 5 h. Waiting for natural cooling, the processed solution was poured into vacuum filtration with organic filter membrane at bottom. Then the system was filtered for several mins till without liquid, and 5 mL GO (~1 mg mL−1) was added into the filter and keep filtering for about 30 min, giving a gel-like AGA-glucose film. After that, the film on organic filter membrane was covered on a piece of copper foil, and then pressed it by metallic plate under a pressure of 1 MPa. Remove the membrane carefully and then a freestanding film was obtained by this way. Put the film into refrigerator at -25°C for 12 h and then carry out vacuum freeze drying for 24 h. Afterwards, the drying film was carbonization in tube furnace at 800°C for 5 h in Ar atmosphere with a heating rate of 1°C min−1. Finally, the AGA-C electrodes were obtained successfully.
AGA electrode. The operation steps are the same as above (AGA-C electrode) except AGA suspension was dispersed in distilled water and performed hydrothermal treat.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CG electrodes and PG electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: CG electrodes. CG powders (Artificial graphite, SAG-R, MTI Co. Ltd), carbon black (ECP-600JD, Lion Specialty Chemicals Co. Ltd.), carboxymethylcellulose (CMC, MTI Co. Ltd) and styrene butadiene rubber (SBR, MTI Co. Ltd) with a weight ratio of 95:2:1.5:1.5 were mixed and dispersed in distilled water to form slurry using a mixer (Thinky, ARE-310). The slurry was coated onto a copper foil with a doctor knife and dried at 60°C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The drying copper foil was punched into small disks as a working electrode in cells.
PG electrodes. The operation steps are the same as above (CG electrodes) except the CG powders was replaced with PG powders (Flake graphite, 100 mesh, Sigma).

LiFePO4 (LFP) powder (MTI Co. Ltd), Super P (ECP-600JD, Lion Specialty Chemicals Co. Ltd.) and Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF, MTI Co. Ltd) with a weight ratio of 8:1:1 were mixed and dispersed in N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) to form slurry using a mixer (Thinky, ARE-310). The slurry was coated onto an aluminum foil with a doctor knife and dried at 60°C for 12 h in a vacuum oven. The drying foil was punched into small disks as a cathode in full cells.

The crystalline phase structure of the products was determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Cu Kα radiation) ranged from 10° to 60° at a scan rate of 6° min−1. The microstructure and morphology of the samples were test by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL JSM-7800F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 Plus, JEM-F200). Raman spectroscopy was carried by Micro-Raman Spectrometer (Technospex, 532 nm). Surface chemical composition data were obtained from X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi).

Electrochemical performance of the anodes was evaluated using coin-type cells CR2032, in which Li-foil was used as a reference electrode and counter electrode, Celgard 2325 microporous membrane was used as separator and 1 mol L−1 LiPF6 dissolved in ethylene carbonate/diethyl carbonate (EC/DEC) with a volume ratio of 1:1 was used as electrolyte. The CR2032 coin cells were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox (Vigor) with O2 and H2O content less than 1 ppm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried by the electrochemical workstation (Bio-Logic, VMP-300). The AC perturbation signal was 10 mV, from 7 MHz to 0.1 Hz. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed in the electrochemical workstation with a potential range of 0.01-1.50 V at a scan rate of 0.3 mV/s. The galvanostatic charge and discharge (GCD) and rate performance tests were carried out at room temperature on LAND CT2001A battery testing system with the cutoff voltage of 0.01-1.50 V. For full cells, AGA-C or CG electrode was used as anode and LFP electrode was used as cathode, in which the capacity of anode was excess of 20% than that of cathode. The GCD was carried out with the cutoff voltage of 2.0-4.0 V.

Chang Zhang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Lei Dong: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Nan Zheng: Data curation, Formal analysis. Haiyin Zhu: Conceptualization. Cong Wu: Conceptualization. Fugang Zhao: Resources. Wei Liu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFA0210600), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21805185) and Shanghai Rising-Star Program (20QA1406600). We also acknowledge Center for High-resolution Electron Microscopy (CℏEM), SPST of ShanghaiTech University (#EM02161943) for support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2021.02.014.
